narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's average. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 97% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed on the bar right away. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar readiness. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 25, significantly lower than the typical 37, which strongly pulled the prediction toward expecting a struggle on the bar. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also much lower than average, added to this concern by highlighting early academic challenges that carried weight in the model's assessment. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success compared to peers. Your middle family income bracket had a minor push toward failure risk, while planning to attend full-time—shared by over 90% of students—provided a small positive influence. Overall, your profile compared to typical students shows promise in some areas like GPA but stands out negatively in key academic metrics like deciles and LSAT, which dominated the decision. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law, but a data-driven flag on bar exam predictors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework, and reapplying once you've addressed these core areas—many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting these improvements. We're rooting for you to turn this around and join the ranks of successful lawyers.
